article.title: Slate Auto Raises $650 Million Series C to Bring a Mid-$20,000 Electric Pickup Truck to Market by Year-End
article.summary: Bezos-backed EV startup Slate Auto closes a $650M Series C led by TWG Global, bringing total funding to $1.4 billion as it prepares to produce a modular electric pickup at a converted Indiana factory with over 160,000 reservations.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Slate Auto, the electric vehicle startup backed by Jeff Bezos, has closed a $650 million Series C funding round as it prepares to begin producing an affordable, modular electric pickup truck at a converted factory in Warsaw, Indiana. The round, led by returning investor TWG Global, brings Slate's total funding to approximately $1.4 billion and is intended to carry the company through to its first customer deliveries in late 2026, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/13/slate-auto-raises-650m-to-fund-its-affordable-ev-truck-plans/).

The company has accumulated over 160,000 refundable reservations for a vehicle it plans to price in the mid-$20,000 range, positioning it as one of the few EV startups targeting the lower end of the American truck market.

## What We Know

TWG Global, a firm led by Guggenheim Partners chief executive and Los Angeles Dodgers owner Mark Walter alongside investor Thomas Tull, led the Series C round. Previous investors include General Catalyst, Bezos's family office, venture firm Slauson & Co., and former Amazon executive Diego Piacentini, as reported by [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/13/slate-auto-raises-650m-to-fund-its-affordable-ev-truck-plans/).

Slate's flagship vehicle is designed around a modular platform that allows the same base truck to be converted between a two-seat pickup, a five-seat SUV, and a fastback configuration through add-on kits priced around $5,000. The base model is deliberately stripped down: it ships with a 150-mile range, no power windows, no infotainment screen, and an unpainted exterior. The company plans to announce official pricing in June 2026, with preorders opening the same month, according to its [press release](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/slate-raises-650-million-in-series-c-round-302739884.html).

Production will take place at a 1.4-million-square-foot former printing plant in Warsaw, Indiana, built in 1958 and now being renovated for EV manufacturing. Slate expects to invest nearly $400 million in the facility, create over 2,000 jobs in Kosciusko County, and contribute up to $39 billion to Indiana's economy over 20 years, according to [Electrek](https://electrek.co/2026/04/13/slate-auto-closes-funding-round-financial-runway-production/).

The company's leadership is drawn heavily from Amazon. Co-founder Jeff Wilke served as Amazon's Consumer CEO before departing in 2021. Peter Faricy, a former Amazon Marketplace vice president, was installed as Slate's CEO in March 2026, replacing Chris Barman, who moved to the role of President of Vehicles. Faricy stated that the Series C will enable the company to reach its "next stages of production this year: on time and on budget," per [Electrek](https://electrek.co/2026/04/13/slate-auto-closes-funding-round-financial-runway-production/).

Barman described the company's pricing ambition as delivering trucks "at nearly half the cost of the average new vehicle," according to the company's [press release](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/slate-raises-650-million-in-series-c-round-302739884.html). The Slate Truck will ship with a NACS charging port, granting access to the Tesla Supercharger network, and customers will be able to use RepairPal's network of over 4,000 service centers for maintenance.

Slate emerged from stealth in April 2025 after three years of secretive development in Troy, Michigan. The company was founded in 2022 and became independent in 2023. It surpassed 100,000 reservations within weeks of its public debut and reached 150,000 by December 2025, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/13/slate-auto-raises-650m-to-fund-its-affordable-ev-truck-plans/).

## What We Don't Know

Slate has not yet disclosed final pricing, a detailed production timeline, or annual output targets for the Warsaw factory. The company's 150-mile base range is modest by current EV standards, and it remains unclear whether higher-range variants will be offered at launch or added later.

The $1.4 billion in total funding is substantial but significantly less than what legacy automakers and better-capitalized EV startups have spent ramping production. Whether Slate can achieve volume manufacturing on this capital base while maintaining its aggressive price point has not been independently verified.

Slate's business model relies heavily on post-purchase customization revenue, but the size and profitability of that aftermarket ecosystem remain theoretical. The company also has not disclosed details about its battery supplier, powertrain specifications beyond range, or crash safety testing status.

## Analysis

Slate's pitch is built on a gap in the American EV market. The average new vehicle transaction price in the United States hovers around $48,000, and most electric trucks from established manufacturers start well above $40,000. A mid-$20,000 electric pickup, if Slate can deliver on that promise, would represent a meaningful entry point for buyers who have been priced out of the EV transition.

The Amazon DNA running through the company's leadership is notable. Slate's approach mirrors Amazon's early retail strategy in several respects: a bare-bones base product designed to undercut competitors on price, with a marketplace of accessories and modifications intended to generate recurring revenue after the initial sale. Whether that e-commerce playbook translates to automotive manufacturing, which demands capital-intensive tooling, regulatory compliance, and supply chain management at a different scale, is the central question facing the company.

The timing of the raise also matters. The expiration of the $7,500 federal EV tax credit in July 2025 forced Slate to adjust its pricing upward from an original target below $20,000. At the same time, EV prices industrywide have been falling, with the gap between electric and gasoline vehicles narrowing to roughly $5,800 according to Kelley Blue Book data. Slate enters a market that is simultaneously more competitive and more price-sensitive than when the company was founded.
